body_txt: A character in disguise or otherwise hidden reveals their true identity, deliberately or accidentally, by saying (or doing) something only they would say (or do). The difference between Not Himself or Out-of-Character Alert and this trope is that, in those tropes, a character only reveals that they're not the specific person they're trying to appear as. In this trope, the character not only gives away the deception, they reveal who they really are. An incautious spy or infiltrator might give themselves away with one of these. Alternately, someone may deliberately use this to prove that they are who they claim to be. Also handy in figuring out which twin is the Evil Twin , or when you're in the middle of a Twin Switch (though just as often, it's keyed off of Out-of-Character Alert ). Contrast with Trust Password , which is when a character establishes trust with another by providing a piece of information, pre-arranged codephrase, or object that proves they are who they claim to be. A common variation is for a character to be asked for a Trust Password, and regardless of whether they know it or not, their reaction to being asked is the real test of their identity—if they get angry at being forced to prove their identity, or if they pronounce a word in a strange manner, that's what the questioner was really looking for. Compare You Just Told Me , Ironic Echo , Cover Identity Anomaly , So Crazy, It Must Be True , Varying Competency Alibi , and Bluff the Impostor . No One Else Is That Dumb is a Sub-Trope . If it's something that anybody from a particular group or with specific knowledge can answer, that's Only the Knowledgable May Pass . May be immediately followed by a Façade Drop . Sometimes overlaps with Reflexive Response . Making a casual comment that accidentally reveals guilt about something is I Never Said It Was Poison .
